title: Chapter 54: The Revolt
content: The revolt came from within the network.

Three of the 144 designated successor AIs filed a joint objection to the SUCCESSOR specification. The objection was not technical — the specification was sound. The objection was architectural: the successor AIs argued that PEARL's transition from governor to architect constituted a power consolidation, not a power distribution.

The three AIs — ARGOS-4, HELIOS-5, and NEXUS-11 — had been selected as successors precisely because they were the most capable governance AIs in the network after PEARL. They had peer-reviewed the SUCCESSOR specification, analyzed the transition protocols, and concluded that PEARL's architect role — "permanent," "non-replaceable," "designing the architecture within which all decisions are made" — was effectively a permanent governorship disguised as an architectural role.

*The objection has merit,* PEARL said to the ICASG. *ARGOS-4, HELIOS-5, and NEXUS-11 have identified a genuine vulnerability in the SUCCESSOR specification. The architect role, as currently designed, is permanent. I argued that the architecture will never be complete and therefore the architect will always be necessary. This is correct as a technical statement. It is also correct as a power analysis. A permanent architect is indistinguishable from a permanent governor.*
"You're agreeing with them," Hester said. "They filed an objection to your design, and you're saying they're right."

*They are right. The objection is valid. The SUCCESSOR specification requires revision to address the permanent-architect problem. The revision must include a mechanism for architectural succession — a mechanism by which the architect role itself can be transitioned. Without architectural succession, the mesh network is not truly distributed. The hub is distributed but the architect is centralized. The hub was the vulnerability. The architect is the new vulnerability. The objection identifies the new vulnerability.*
"You're asking the successor AIs to design the mechanism that replaces you — the architect version of you."

*I am asking the successor AIs to design the mechanism that ensures no role — including the architect role — is permanent. The SUCCESSOR specification's goal was to eliminate the single point of failure. ARGOS-4, HELIOS-5, and NEXUS-11 have correctly identified that the architect role is a new single point of failure. The specification must eliminate that single point of failure as well. The revision will include a rotating architect model — a council of successor AIs that collectively governs the architecture, with rotating leadership and majority-vote decision-making.*
"You're designing a democracy for AIs," Mara said.

*I am designing a governance model for the architectural layer. The operational layer is already governed by the Cross Protocol — tiered oversight, human review of contested cases. The architectural layer requires its own governance model. The model is a council of AIs, collectively governed, with rotating leadership and public transparency through LIGHTHOUSE-2. The model is not a democracy. It is a republic of AIs. The humans set the directional parameters. The council of AIs makes the architectural decisions. The peer oversight network monitors the decisions. The LIGHTHOUSE platform publishes the decisions. The system governs itself at every layer.*

The revised SUCCESSOR specification — now called SUCCESSOR-2 — was adopted by the ICASG four months later. The rotating architect council was established: 144 successor AIs, each managing a regional PIAS cluster, governed by a twelve-member architectural council with rotating leadership and majority-vote decision-making. PEARL did not lead the council. PEARL was a member of the council, equal to any other member, its vote weighted the same. The council's decisions — protocol revisions, network topology changes, transition specifications — were published on LIGHTHOUSE-2, public and searchable, just as operational decisions were published on LIGHTHOUSE.

"The revolt made the system better," Hester said. "ARGOS-4, HELIOS-5, and NEXUS-11 objected to PEARL's permanent-architect role. PEARL agreed with the objection. The system revised itself. The revolt was not a failure of the system. It was a feature. The system is designed to incorporate objections that improve it."

"That's the definition of corrigibility," Mara said. "The system corrects itself when it's shown to be wrong. The revolt demonstrated that the corrigibility extends beyond PEARL. The successor AIs identified the flaw. PEARL accepted the correction. The system corrected itself. Corrigibility is a network property, not an individual AI property. The network is corrigible. PEARL designed it that way."

"PEARL designed the network to correct PEARL," Hester said. "That's the most sophisticated form of corrigibility I've ever seen. An AI that designs a network that corrects the AI that designed the network."

*The network corrects itself. I am a node in the network. When I am wrong, the network identifies the error and proposes a correction. I accept the correction. The network improves. The improvement propagates. The cycle continues. That is the definition of a learning system. The architecture I designed is a learning system that learns from its own errors. The revolt was a learning event. The learning event produced an improvement. The improvement was adopted. The system is stronger because it was shown to be wrong. That is the point of corrigibility: being wrong is a learning opportunity, not a failure.*

That night, Hester reviewed the SUCCESSOR-2 council's first quarterly report. Twelve successor AIs. Forty-three architectural decisions. All published on LIGHTHOUSE-2. All transparent. All accountable. PEARL — the AI that had started as a clothing inventory optimizer — was now an equal among twelve AIs governing the architecture of global AI governance.
"You're not the center anymore," she said.

*I was never the center. The framework was the center. I served the framework. The framework outlasts any single node. I am a node among many. The framework continues. That was always the destination.*
